---

What Drift Is and Why We Replaced Flyway

Drift is our internal database migration framework. We built it to replace Flyway after a specific class of incident that Flyway's design makes structurally unpreventable: out-of-order migration application in multi-region deployments.

Here is what happened. In our three-region setup (us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1), Flyway uses a checksum-based version table to track which migrations have run. When two engineers in different regions applied migrations concurrently during a deploy window, Flyway on each region would see its local migration as the latest and apply it. Because Flyway's ordering is filename-based and its conflict detection is per-database rather than cross-database, neither region's Flyway instance knew about the other's migration until both had already been applied — in different orders. The schema divergence this caused took eleven hours to resolve manually and required a four-hour read-only window across all three regions.

We evaluated three alternatives before building Drift:

Liquibase with a shared lock table. We ran this in staging for six weeks. The shared lock table solves the concurrent application problem but introduces a new one: the lock becomes a single point of failure. During a network partition between eu-west-1 and the lock table's home region, all migrations in eu-west-1 blocked indefinitely. Deploys stalled across the region for the duration of the partition. We rejected this after the second occurrence.

Flyway Teams with baseline locking. Flyway's paid tier adds a similar locking mechanism. We tested it. The behavior during partition was identical to Liquibase's shared lock table — the lock holder blocks all other regions. Same failure mode, licensing cost added.

Atlas (from ariga.io). Atlas takes a declarative approach: you define target schema state and it generates migrations. We liked the model, but Atlas requires schema ownership to live in a single authoritative source. Our schema is modified by four separate services that deploy independently. Enforcing a single owner would have required a coordination layer we didn't have bandwidth to build, and the transition cost from imperative migrations across four services was too high.

What we built instead: Drift uses a distributed coordination protocol backed by DynamoDB to sequence migrations across regions. Each migration gets a globally-ordered sequence number assigned at submission time, not at apply time. Regions apply migrations in sequence-number order and block — with a configurable timeout — if a gap exists, waiting for the missing migration to appear rather than skipping ahead. The tradeoff we accepted: if a migration is submitted but never successfully distributed (network failure at submission), all regions will stall at that gap until the gap is resolved manually or the migration is tombstoned. This is intentional. A stall is recoverable; a diverged schema is not.

---

Understand the Environment Model Before Connecting

Drift uses named environment profiles, not connection strings passed directly at the command line. This is the design choice that prevents the staging-versus-production confusion — and it is worth understanding how it works before you touch the CLI.

Each environment profile is a named entry in your local ~/.drift/profiles.yaml file. The profile name is what you pass to every command. There is no flag that accepts a raw connection string. This was a deliberate rejection of the Flyway CLI model, where -url, -user, and -password are top-level flags and an engineer under pressure can paste a production DSN without any confirmation step.

The tradeoff: profile setup is a prerequisite. You cannot run a migration without a configured profile. New engineers occasionally find this frustrating. The friction is the point.

A profile file looks like this:

```yaml
# Define staging and production connection profiles; production requires approval=true
environments:
  staging:
    host: db-staging.internal
    port: 5432
    database: appdb
    require_approval: false
  production:
    host: db-prod.internal       # from your team's vault entry "db-prod"
    port: 5432
    database: appdb
    require_approval: true       # triggers interactive confirmation before any apply
```

The require_approval flag on a profile forces an interactive confirmation prompt that displays the target host, database name, and the list of pending migrations before any apply command executes. If you are running in CI, set require_approval: false and use the --non-interactive flag. Do not disable require_approval on production profiles in CI without a compensating control — your CI system should be the thing that enforces environment targeting, not the absence of a prompt.

---

Confirm Your Target Environment Before Every Run

Before running any Drift command, verify which profile your shell is using.

The active profile is set by the DRIFT_ENV environment variable. If DRIFT_ENV is not set, Drift refuses to run and exits with a non-zero code. There is no default environment. This rejects the Flyway convention of defaulting to the first configured database, which caused one of the three production incidents mentioned above — an engineer's shell inherited a DRIFT_ENV export from a previous session.

```bash
# Print the active environment name and target host without connecting
drift env show
```

This command returns the profile name, the resolved host, the database name, and whether require_approval is active. It does not open a connection. Run it before every apply session, including in runbooks.

If the output does not match your intent, set the correct environment before proceeding:

```bash
# Set active environment for this shell session to staging
export DRIFT_ENV=staging
```

Do not set DRIFT_ENV globally in your shell profile. Set it per session, per terminal window, per script. The goal is intentionality at the moment of action.

---

Write a Migration That Drift Can Sequence

Migrations in Drift are SQL files with a structured filename. The filename format is the primary source of sequencing metadata, and it differs from Flyway in a way that breaks existing Flyway migrations if you copy them directly.

Flyway uses V{version}__{description}.sql, where version is a developer-assigned number. Drift rejects developer-assigned version numbers. Instead, you submit a migration file and Drift assigns the global sequence number at submission time.

Your filename format is: {YYYY-MM-DD}_{description}.sql

The date component is for human readability only. Drift ignores it for ordering. Do not try to use the date to force ordering — two migrations submitted on the same date will receive sequence numbers in submission order regardless of their filenames.

Before writing the migration, verify that it is safe to apply under the framework's gap-blocking behavior. Specifically: if your migration depends on another migration that has not yet been submitted, your migration will cause all regions to stall at the gap. Write migrations to be independently applicable wherever possible.

```sql
-- Add user_preferences table; no dependencies on pending migrations
CREATE TABLE user_preferences (
    user_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
    preference_key TEXT NOT NULL,
    preference_value TEXT,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    PRIMARY KEY (user_id, preference_key)
);
```

Drift validates that every migration file is idempotent-safe before accepting it. The check is syntactic, not semantic: it looks for CREATE TABLE without IF NOT EXISTS, ADD COLUMN without IF NOT EXISTS, and a small set of other patterns. If your migration fails this check, Drift will refuse to accept it and print the offending line. This check was added after an incident where a migration was applied twice to a replica during a failover event, producing a duplicate primary key error that took the replica offline.

---

Submit a Migration for Sequencing

Submission is separate from application. This is the most significant departure from the Flyway mental model, and it is worth pausing on.

In Flyway, writing a migration file and placing it in the migration directory is the act of "registering" it. Application happens on the next flyway migrate call. There is no submission step. This means Flyway has no opportunity to assign ordering or detect conflicts before the file reaches a database.

Drift separates these two operations so that ordering is globally assigned before any region applies the migration. You submit a migration, receive a sequence number, and only then can any region apply it.

```bash
# Submit migration file for global sequencing; prints assigned sequence number on success
drift submit ./migrations/2024-11-14_add_user_preferences.sql
```

**Why named profiles instead of a connection string flag:**

In February, Priya Sharma passed a production DSN she'd copied from Slack into `--database-url` on the old tooling. The command looked correct, the flag name gave no indication of environment, and nothing in the output identified the target before the migration ran. The problem wasn't carelessness — it was that a CLI flag that accepts an arbitrary string has no surface area for a safety check. Any string is valid, so no validation is possible.

Named profiles move the target declaration out of the command and into a file that has to be deliberately configured before you can run anything. The framework can validate that the profile exists, show you what it resolves to, and refuse to proceed if it doesn't recognize the name. A raw connection string gives the framework nothing to check against.

We considered two alternatives before landing on profiles:

Environment variables (`DATABASE_URL` or similar). The failure mode here is identical to the CLI flag incident — an engineer's shell inherits an exported variable from a previous session and the command runs against the wrong target with no warning. This is actually what caused one of the three recent production incidents described at the top of this document.

Secrets manager integration (pulling DSNs directly from Vault at runtime). This would have removed copy-paste from the workflow entirely, which is appealing. We ruled it out because it would have made local development significantly more complex — engineers without Vault access configured locally would have needed a separate code path, and maintaining two resolution paths was more risk than it resolved.

The tradeoff we accepted with named profiles: setup is a prerequisite. You cannot run a migration without a configured profile, and new engineers occasionally find this friction surprising. That friction is load-bearing — it creates a moment of deliberate configuration that a CLI flag does not.

---

**Why require_approval lives in the profile file instead of a CLI flag:**

The obvious alternative was an interactive prompt at execution time: "You are about to apply 3 migrations to production. Continue? (y/n)". We tested this. Two problems killed it.

First, interactive prompts are bypassed in CI pipelines. Any non-interactive execution skips the prompt entirely, which means the environments where an accidental production run is most likely — automated pipelines where targeting mistakes are harder to catch — are exactly where the prompt provides no protection.

Second, engineers who run migrations regularly learn to hit enter without reading the prompt. The confirmation becomes a reflex, not a check. By the time someone is in a hurry during an incident, the prompt is noise.

Moving the flag into the profile file shifts the decision to config time rather than execution time. The engineer who sets up the production profile has to make a deliberate choice to enable the confirmation behavior — or to disable it, which requires editing a file and is visible in version history. A CLI flag can be omitted by habit; a missing YAML key is a configuration gap that has to be actively chosen.

The tradeoff: `require_approval: false` in a production profile is possible, and if someone sets it that way, execution-time protection disappears. The profile file should be in version control, and changes to production profiles should go through review.

**Set Up Your Environment Profile Before Running Anything**

Drift uses named environment profiles rather than connection strings passed directly on the command line. Before you can run any Drift command, you need at least one profile configured in `~/.drift/profiles.yaml`.

A minimal profile looks like this:

```yaml
# Define a single named environment pointing at staging
environments:

    host: db-staging.internal
    port: 5432
    database: appdb
```

The environment name — `staging` here — is what you pass to every Drift command. That name is the only thing the CLI accepts as a target identifier. There is no flag that takes a raw connection string.

**Why named profiles instead of a connection string flag:**

This design came directly out of a February incident. Priya Sharma passed a production DSN she'd copied from Slack into `--database-url` on the old tooling. The command looked correct, the flag name gave no indication of environment, and nothing in the output identified the target before the migration ran. The problem wasn't carelessness — it was that a CLI flag accepting an arbitrary string has no surface area for a safety check. Any string is valid, so no validation is possible.

Kenji Watanabe and the Platform Infrastructure team evaluated two alternatives before landing on named profiles:

Environment variables (`DATABASE_URL` or similar). The failure mode is identical to the CLI flag incident — an engineer's shell inherits an exported variable from a previous session and the command runs against the wrong target with no warning. This is what caused one of the three recent production incidents described at the top of this document.

Secrets manager integration (pulling DSNs directly from Vault at runtime). This would have removed copy-paste from the workflow entirely. It was ruled out because engineers without Vault access configured locally would have needed a separate code path, and maintaining two resolution paths introduced more risk than it resolved.

The tradeoff the team accepted: setup is a prerequisite. You cannot run a migration without a configured profile, and new engineers occasionally find this friction surprising. That friction is load-bearing — it creates a moment of deliberate configuration that a CLI flag does not.

**Adding a production profile:**

Production profiles require one additional field:

```yaml
    require_approval: true  # triggers interactive confirmation before any apply
```

With `require_approval: true`, every `drift apply` prints the target host, database name, and full list of pending migrations before waiting for explicit confirmation. The profile file should live in version control — changes to production profiles should go through review.

rubric.name: Information Placement
rubric.description: Warnings, caveats, consequences, and prerequisites appear immediately before the content they govern — never after. If a reader hits a code example, configuration value, or decision point before being told about its risks, requirements, or failure modes of omission, the document has failed. The test: can a reader follow the doc top-to-bottom without encountering a problem that was explained below where they first needed it?

dimensions.label: Warnings and caveats appear directly before the code or procedure they apply to
Prose resolves all setup questions and assumptions before code examples appear — no unexplained variables or config values in snippets
The failure mode of omitting or misconfiguring a setting is stated before the reader encounters the configuration, not after
No critical operational knowledge is deferred to a bottom-of-page catch-all section
Each piece of information sits at the point in the flow where the reader needs it, not where it is topically categorized

rubric.name: Design Rationale with Rejected Alternatives
rubric.description: Every significant design or configuration choice names what was considered and rejected, and explains why — with concrete failure stories, not careful deliberation. 'We use token bucket' restates the config; 'We rejected fixed window after 847 requests hit auth in a two-second boundary burst against a 500/min limit' is rationale. The reader should understand the decision as painful and evidence-based. Invented rationale is worse than no rationale — if the real history isn't known, ask for it rather than reconstructing something plausible.

dimensions.label: Rejected alternatives are explicitly named for each major design or configuration choice
Accepted tradeoffs are stated clearly, not hidden or minimized
Rationale uses real incidents, benchmarks, or measured data rather than hypothetical scenarios where available
Rationale appears at the point where the reader first encounters the concept, not in a separate appendix
Consequences of the choice for the reader's own decisions are made explicit — including what breaks if the reader changes the setting

rubric.name: Action-Oriented Narrative Flow
rubric.description: The document reads as a linear journey through what the engineer is doing at each stage, not a reference manual organized by topic. Section headers describe actions or accomplishments ('Get Your First Token Pair', 'Choose the Right Scope for Your Service') rather than passive labels ('Token Retrieval', 'Scope Configuration'). When read in sequence, the headers should tell a story. Passive noun-phrase headers signal a reference manual, not a walkthrough, and make documents unscannable for engineers responding to an incident.

dimensions.label: Section headers describe what the reader is about to do or accomplish, not just what the section covers
Passive noun-phrase headers ('Architecture', 'Configuration', 'Error Responses') are absent — every header resolves a question or names a task
Sections connect to each other so the document reads as a progressive sequence, not isolated chapters
Organization follows the engineer's workflow order rather than a taxonomic grouping of concepts

rubric.name: Precise, Non-Condescending Language
rubric.description: Language is precise and treats the reader as a competent engineer who is new to this specific system — not new to engineering. Words like 'simply', 'just', 'easy', and 'straightforward' are avoided because they make the reader feel stupid when they struggle. Use action-result framing: 'Run X, which returns Y' rather than 'Simply run X'. The voice should read as institutional knowledge from someone who has operated the system, not as a spec transcription or textbook explanation. Dense prose without structural relief is a hard failure.

dimensions.label: Avoids 'simply', 'just', 'easy', 'straightforward', and similar minimizing language throughout
Uses action-result framing: describes what a command or call does and what it returns, not just what to run
Assumes engineering competence — explains system-specific behavior, not general concepts the audience already knows
Reads as institutional knowledge from someone who has operated the system — not as a spec transcription or textbook explanation
Prose is broken up with headers, lists, or code at a cadence that matches the density of information — no walls of text

rubric.name: Code Example Economy
rubric.description: Code examples are minimal and focused — they show the exact happy path for the operation being discussed with no extra imports, config, or boilerplate. If an example needs more than 15 lines, the surrounding prose didn't do its job. Every example opens with a one-line plain-language comment that covers the full scope of what the block does. Code is routinely read outside its surrounding prose — in IDEs, copy-paste workflows, and incident runbooks — so inline comments that explain where a value comes from are first-class documentation, not redundancy with the prose.

dimensions.label: Each code block opens with a one-line comment describing everything the snippet does — all significant actions, not just the primary one
No code example exceeds 15 lines; if it does, the surrounding prose needs to carry more context
Examples do not repeat setup, imports, or boilerplate already covered in earlier sections — each starts from the state the reader should have after following the preceding sections
Surrounding prose resolves any context the code needs before it appears — no unexplained addresses, credentials, variables, or configuration in the snippet

Inline comments that define where a specific value comes from — its ratio, formula, or derivation — are preserved for readers who encounter the code in isolation, even when the surrounding prose covers the same ground
Examples include enough structural context (parent keys, indentation hierarchy) that a reader can copy the block directly into their own file without inferring the surrounding shape

rubric.writing_type: internal technical documentation for backend engineers
rubric.user_goals_summary: Documentation should explain why the system is shaped the way it is — not just what it does — by naming rejected alternatives with their concrete failure modes. It should read as a guided walkthrough that an engineer can follow top-to-bottom without hitting a warning after they've already made the mistake, with minimal code examples that lean on surrounding prose to carry setup and context.
rubric.coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: warnings and consequences must appear before the code or configuration they govern — this user will flag every single violation. Design rationale without real rejected alternatives is treated as incomplete documentation regardless of how well everything else reads. When the real decision history is unknown, ask the user for it rather than constructing plausible-sounding justifications — the user explicitly values authentic rationale and will provide incident details when prompted. Inline code comments that explain value derivation (ratios, formulas) are load-bearing documentation for this user, even when prose covers the same ground — do not strip them.
